title: Te Kaitiakitanga o te Awa Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native bush pigeon (kererū) that once flourished in its surrounding forests, and its waters have sustained our community for generations. Students will apply mathematical reasoning to understand and address real environmental challenges facing our awa.
learningObjectives: Calculate and interpret water quality indices using pH, nitrate, and turbidity data collected from the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Create and analyze statistical representations (box plots, scatter plots) to identify patterns and correlations in stream health data
Apply geometric principles to calculate stream flow rates and catchment areas using topographic maps of our local whenua
Develop mathematical models to predict environmental impacts and propose data-informed solutions for stream restoration

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa (Connecting with the Stream)
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with kaumātua to learn its pūrākau (stories) and cultural significance. They collect water samples from three strategic locations while recording observational data and geographical coordinates.

activities.title: Tātari Raraunga Wai (Water Data Analysis)
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students measure pH, nitrate levels, and turbidity. They create comparative statistical representations (box plots, scatter plots) to analyze variations between sampling locations and identify potential pollution sources.

activities.title: Te Au o te Awa (Flow of the Stream)
activities.description: Students apply geometric formulas to calculate stream cross-sectional area and flow rate. Using topographic maps, they calculate the catchment area that feeds into the Mangakōtukutuku Stream and explore how land use mathematics affects water volume.

activities.title: He Rautaki Whakaora (Restoration Strategy)
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop mathematical models to predict how proposed restoration interventions (riparian planting, wetland creation) might improve water quality indicators over time, presenting their findings with supporting calculations.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and environmental kaitiaki for cultural guidance and historical knowledge
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality testing kits and monitoring protocols
LINZ Topo50 maps (BK37) of the Mangakōtukutuku catchment area
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' online resources and data analysis tools

tasks: Water quality data analysis report with statistical representations
Written explanation of mathematical calculations for stream flow and catchment area
Proposal for stream restoration with predictive mathematical modeling
Reflection on how mathematics supports kaitiakitanga of local waterways

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics: Statistical investigation, Probability, Equations and expressions, Geometric representations
Science: Living World, Planet Earth and Beyond
Social Sciences: Place and Environment, Continuity and Change
